codeReview.summary: Clean refactor that successfully removes all font knowledge from the compositor and encapsulates glyph cache construction in CpuBackend::new(). The compositor is reduced from 459 to 174 lines with zero fonts:: imports, zero Content:: references, and zero extern crate fonts. Core render loop logic (scene read → render → present) is ~27 lines, within the <=30 line target. CpuBackend::new() correctly heap-allocates caches and the backend struct itself to avoid stack overflow. QEMU visual verification was performed by the worker. All 5 expectedBehavior claims verified. Three non-blocking issues found: missing SAFETY comments on new unsafe blocks in CpuBackend::new().

issues.description: Missing // SAFETY: comment on unsafe block for mono GlyphCache heap allocation via alloc_zeroed + Box::from_raw. Project protocol requires SAFETY comments on all unsafe blocks. Should document: layout is correct for GlyphCache, alloc_zeroed returns valid zeroed memory, Box::from_raw takes ownership of the allocation.
Missing // SAFETY: comment on unsafe block for proportional GlyphCache heap allocation (same pattern as mono cache above).

Missing // SAFETY: comment on unsafe block for CpuBackend heap allocation. This is the most complex unsafe block — uses alloc_zeroed + ptr::write for Drop-bearing fields + Box::from_raw. Should document: (1) layout is correct for CpuBackend, (2) ptr::write is used for fields with Drop impls (mono_cache, prop_cache, pool, damage) because alloc_zeroed memory is bitwise-zero but not semantically initialized for these types, (3) prev_bounds is valid as all-zeros (tuple of integers), (4) Box::from_raw takes ownership after all fields are initialized.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md states 'unsafe blocks: Require // SAFETY: comments' and CLAUDE.md has the same rule, but CpuBackend::new() was added with 3 unsafe blocks and zero SAFETY comments. The compositor rewrite also has ~10 unsafe blocks with only 1 SAFETY comment (line 71). Most compositor unsafe blocks are pre-existing patterns that were carried forward in the rewrite, but the render library blocks are entirely new code. The convention is documented but not consistently enforced during refactors.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/libraries/render/lib.rs lines 115, 130, 152 — all new unsafe blocks, zero SAFETY comments. system/services/compositor/main.rs lines 44, 52, 57, 77, 89, 91, 92, 96, 97, 98 — unsafe blocks without SAFETY comments (only line 71 has one).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker's skillFeedback suggests adding stack overflow risk awareness to the refactor-worker skill for no_std/bare-metal environments. CpuBackend contains a 6 KiB prev_bounds array that caused stack overflow when returned by value on the compositor's 16 KB user-space stack. Worker correctly solved this with heap allocation (Box<CpuBackend>) but the skill has no guidance about this bare-metal constraint.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff discoveredIssues[0] documents the stack overflow. skillFeedback.suggestedChanges[0]: 'The refactor-worker skill could mention stack overflow risks when moving large structs between function boundaries in no_std/bare-metal environments, where stack sizes are typically much smaller than hosted environments.'

summary: PASS. The compositor is successfully minimized to 174 lines with zero font knowledge — no fonts:: imports, no Content:: references, no extern crate fonts. Glyph cache construction is fully encapsulated in CpuBackend::new() in the render library. Core render loop logic is ~27 lines (within <=30 target). Worker performed QEMU visual verification confirming pixel-identical output. Build and all tests pass. Three non-blocking issues: missing SAFETY comments on new unsafe blocks in CpuBackend::new(). The Box<CpuBackend> return type to avoid stack overflow is a reasonable solution for the bare-metal 16 KB stack constraint.
